section.title: The Oracle Against Israel
section.sectionRationale: The trap set by the oracles against the nations now snaps shut. The same formula, for three transgressions, even four, turns at last on Israel itself, and the crimes are not distant war atrocities but the everyday injustice, greed, and sacrilege of God's own covenant people, made worse by all the grace He had poured out on them.
section.thesis: Israel's polished religion masks the sale of the poor, the trampling of the needy, and the silencing of God's word; and against people so richly graced, judgment will fall so heavily that not even the strongest can escape.
section.frameNote: Three strokes: the indictment of injustice and sacrilege; the reminder of God's saving grace they despised; and the sentence that leaves no warrior able to flee.

units.label: The charge against Israel
units.note: the poor sold, the needy trampled

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “For three transgressions of Israel, even four, I will not revoke My judgment, because they sell the {{tsaddiq|righteous}} for silver and the needy for a pair of sandals.
They trample on the heads of the poor as on the dust of the earth; they push the needy out of their way. A man and his father have relations with the same girl and so profane My holy name.
They lie down beside every altar on garments taken in pledge. And in the house of their God, they drink wine obtained through fines.

units.label: The grace they despised
units.note: I destroyed the Amorite, I brought you up from Egypt

verses.text: Yet it was I who destroyed the Amorite before them, though his height was like that of the cedars, and he was as strong as the oaks. Yet I destroyed his fruit above and his roots below.
And I brought you up from the land of Egypt and led you forty years in the wilderness, that you might take possession of the land of the Amorite.
I raised up prophets from your sons and {{nazir|Nazirites}} from your young men. Is this not true, O children of Israel?” declares the LORD.
“But you made the Nazirites drink wine and commanded the prophets not to prophesy.

units.label: The sentence: no escape
units.note: the swift, the strong, and the brave all fail

verses.text: Behold, I am about to crush you in your place as with a cart full of grain.
Escape will fail the swift, the strong will not prevail by his strength, and the mighty will not save his life.
The archer will not stand his ground, the fleet of foot will not escape, and the horseman will not save his life.
Even the bravest of mighty men will flee naked on that day,” declares the LORD.

tsaddiq.gloss: The righteous or innocent person, one in the right. In the courts and markets of Israel, the tsaddiq was being sold for silver, condemned by bribed judges and creditors for the price of a trivial debt. The word exposes that Israel's injustice was not just unkindness but the deliberate crushing of the innocent for profit.

nazir.gloss: A Nazirite, someone specially consecrated to the LORD by a vow that included abstaining from wine, Numbers 6. God raised up Nazirites and prophets as living gifts of His word and holiness to Israel; the people repaid Him by forcing the Nazirites to break their vow and silencing the prophets, spurning the very grace meant to keep them.

basic.context: After circling all the neighboring nations, God now turns on Israel itself. The crimes are not foreign war atrocities but everyday injustice at home: selling poor people into debt slavery, trampling the needy, sexual sin, and abusing worship. Worse, all of this came from people God had rescued from Egypt and blessed with prophets. So the sentence is that no strength, speed, or courage will save them from the coming collapse.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the climax the whole opening was building toward. God applies the same measure to His own people that He applied to the pagans, and then heavier, because they had received so much grace. The message is that religious ceremony cannot cover social injustice, and privilege does not exempt anyone from God's justice.
basic.originalLanguages: The victims are the tsaddiq, the righteous or innocent, sold for silver and even for a pair of sandals. God recalls that He raised up nazir, Nazirites, and prophets as gifts of His holiness and word, only for Israel to corrupt the Nazirites and silence the prophets. The verdict is total: even the mighty will flee naked.
basic.typology: The innocent one sold for silver foreshadows Christ, the truly Righteous One, betrayed for thirty pieces of silver and handed over by His own people. Israel's rejection of the prophets anticipates the rejection of the Prophet greater than them all.
basic.christ: The righteous sold for silver points straight to Jesus, the Righteous One betrayed for silver and trampled by the powerful. Where Israel silenced the prophets, they would finally crucify the Word made flesh. Yet the Judge who condemns injustice is the Savior who bore that judgment so the guilty could go free.
basic.god: God cares fiercely about how the poor and vulnerable are treated; He counts their oppression as sin against His holy name. He remembers the grace He has given and is grieved when it is despised. And His justice cannot be bribed, dodged, or outrun.
basic.crossReferences: James 5 warns the rich who defraud their workers. Matthew 26 records the Righteous One sold for silver. Isaiah 1 shows God rejecting worship that coexists with injustice.
basic.oneStory: This oracle exposes that God's own people stand condemned along with the nations, deepening the need for a rescue only God can provide. The Righteous One sold for silver, foreshadowed here, will be the very One who saves the unrighteous.

context.literary: The oracle uses the identical formula as the seven that precede it, so that Israel is caught in the same net. But the pattern then expands: instead of a brief crime and a fire sentence, Amos lingers over a catalogue of social sins, recounts the history of grace Israel despised, and closes with a sixfold picture of military collapse. The lengthening shows this is the true target of the whole passage.
context.historical: Under Jeroboam II, Israel enjoyed wealth and expansion, but the prosperity was built on the backs of the poor. Debt slavery for trivial sums, corrupt courts, garments seized in pledge and kept overnight against the law, Deuteronomy 24, and fines funneled into feasting, all mark a society where the strong preyed on the weak while keeping up a busy religious calendar.
context.canonical: The passage stands with the great prophetic protests that true covenant faithfulness is inseparable from justice for the vulnerable, Isaiah 1, Micah 6:8, Jeremiah 7. It also grounds the New Testament theme that privilege and knowledge increase accountability, and that the Righteous One would be sold and rejected by His own.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To turn the accumulated moral weight of the oracles onto Israel, to strip away the illusion that ceremonial religion excuses social sin, and to declare an inescapable judgment on a people who despised the grace that made them.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The oracle describes Israel's sins and the coming judgment; it prescribes nothing cruel to imitate. Its abiding claims are that God defends the poor, that worship without justice is offensive to Him, and that received grace deepens responsibility.

debates.question: Are the social sins here mainly about individual acts or systemic injustice?

views.label: Personal wrongdoing
views.case: The text names concrete acts by concrete people: selling a person, keeping a pledged garment, exploiting a girl, so the call is to personal repentance and integrity.

views.label: Structural oppression
views.case: The crimes describe a whole social order, corrupt courts, debt systems, and religious institutions, that grinds the poor down, so repentance must reshape structures, not just hearts.

debates.bearing: Scripture holds both together: God addresses real persons and the systems they build. Genuine repentance changes individual conduct and confronts the unjust structures we benefit from.

study.originalLanguages: The heart of the charge is the sale of the tsaddiq, the righteous or innocent person, for silver and even for the price of a pair of sandals, a trivial debt turned into a life owned. To trample the poor and profane God's holy name in the same breath shows that injustice and irreverence are two sides of one coin. Against this, God recalls the nazir, the Nazirite set apart to Him, and the prophets, both of them living gifts of His holiness and His word; Israel repaid the gift by making the Nazirite drink and silencing the prophet. The closing sentence hammers with six clauses of failure, the swift, the strong, the archer, the runner, the horseman, the brave, so that no human resource remains.

typology.type: The righteous one sold for silver, 2:6
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Righteous One, betrayed for thirty pieces of silver and delivered up by His own people, Matthew 26:14-16, Acts 3:14.
The rejection of the prophets that culminates in the killing of the Prophet greater than Moses, Christ Himself, Matthew 21:33-39, Hebrews 1:1-2.

study.christ: Israel sold the righteous for silver and silenced God's messengers; both crimes reach their terrible fulfillment in Jesus. He is the truly Righteous One, betrayed for silver, trampled by the powerful, and handed over by His own nation. He is the Word the world tried to silence at the cross. Yet the astonishing turn of the gospel is that the Judge who here condemns injustice is Himself the innocent victim, and He bore the crushing sentence, the judgment no strength could escape, so that the guilty might be spared. The verdict of Amos 2 falls on Christ so that mercy can reach us.
study.god: The LORD is the defender of the poor and the innocent; He counts their exploitation as a direct assault on His holy name, so that worship and injustice can never be separated in His sight. He remembers His own grace, the exodus, the conquest, the gift of prophets and Nazirites, and treats its rejection as a grievous wrong. And His justice is inescapable: He will crush the guilty in their place, and no speed, strength, or courage can outrun Him.

crossReferences.note: The law forbidding keeping a poor man's pledged garment overnight, the very sin of Amos 2:8.
What the LORD requires: to act justly, love mercy, and walk humbly, the covenant faithfulness Israel abandoned.
The Righteous One sold for silver, fulfilling the pattern of Amos 2:6.
A New Testament echo warning the rich who defraud their laborers and condemn the innocent.

study.oneStory: The trap closes: God's own covenant people are condemned alongside the nations, so that the whole world is shown guilty and in need of rescue. The image of the righteous sold for silver quietly points ahead to the Righteous One who would be sold and crushed, bearing the inescapable sentence Himself, so that the story of judgment could open into a story of salvation.

quiz.q: Why is the oracle against Israel the climax of the opening chapters?

options.text: God applies the same formula used on the nations to His own people, and heavier, because of the grace they despised.
options.note: Israel is the true target; privilege increases accountability.

options.text: It is the longest oracle in the section.
options.note: Its length signals the emphasis, but the point is that God's own people are condemned too.

options.text: It is the only oracle that mentions specific sins.
options.note: Every oracle names sins; Israel's are simply detailed at length.

options.text: It shows God finally taking Israel's side against the nations.
options.note: The opposite: Israel is folded into the same judgment as the nations.

quiz.q: What does selling the righteous for silver and the needy for a pair of sandals reveal?

options.text: The innocent poor were sold into debt slavery for trivial sums by a corrupt, greedy society.
options.note: Human lives were traded for the price of a small debt.

options.text: Israel had a thriving sandal and silver trade.
options.note: It describes exploitation of the poor, not commerce.

options.text: The courts and creditors preyed on the vulnerable for profit.
options.note: Right about the exploitation; the sting is that a whole person was sold for a trivial debt.

options.text: People simply could not afford basic goods.
options.note: The charge is active oppression, not mere poverty.

quiz.q: How had Israel treated the grace God gave them?

options.text: They corrupted the Nazirites and silenced the prophets, spurning the gifts of God's holiness and word.
options.note: They repaid grace with contempt for the very means of grace.

options.text: They were simply ungrateful and forgetful.
options.note: True, but the text names active corruption of Nazirites and prophets.

options.text: They kept the ceremonies but neglected the poor a little.
options.note: The charge is far graver: crushing the poor and muzzling God's messengers.

options.text: They honored the prophets and obeyed the law.
options.note: They did the reverse, commanding the prophets not to prophesy.

quiz.q: What does the sixfold picture of collapse in verses 14-16 teach?

options.text: No human strength, speed, or courage can save anyone from God's judgment.
options.note: Every human resource fails; the sentence is inescapable.

options.text: Israel's army needed better training.
options.note: The point is the futility of all human strength before God, not military reform.

options.text: Even the best soldiers will be overwhelmed on that day.
options.note: Right, and the deeper point is that no human resource whatever can avert God's judgment.

options.text: Only cowards will flee, while the brave will stand.
options.note: Even the bravest will flee naked; courage is no exception.

quiz.q: How does the righteous sold for silver point to Christ?

options.text: Jesus, the Righteous One, was betrayed for silver and crushed by the very judgment Amos describes, so the guilty could go free.
options.note: The innocent victim bears the inescapable sentence in our place.

options.text: It reminds us that betrayal is a common human sin.
options.note: True generally, but Scripture ties this specifically to Christ sold for silver.

options.text: Jesus was also rejected by His own people, like the prophets here.
options.note: Right about the rejection; the fuller point is the Righteous One sold for silver bearing judgment.

options.text: It has no connection to Jesus at all.
options.note: The New Testament draws the line straight from this image to Christ.

questions: God counts the exploitation of the poor as sin against His holy name. Where might your comfort or convenience rest on someone else being treated unjustly?
Israel kept up busy worship while trampling the needy. How honest are you about the gap between your religious life and your treatment of people?
They silenced the prophets and corrupted the Nazirites, spurning the means of grace. Are there ways God speaks that you quietly try to mute in your own life?
No strength, speed, or courage could avert the judgment. What are you tempted to rely on to save yourself instead of the mercy of God?
The Righteous One was sold for silver to save the unrighteous. How does it move you that the Judge of Amos 2 became the victim who bore your sentence?

translationNotes.note: For a pair of sandals may indicate either a trivial debt or a corrupt legal transaction sealed by the exchange of a sandal; either way, a human life is traded for almost nothing.
The same girl renders a phrase whose exact reference is debated, a cult prostitute, a servant girl, or a betrothed woman; the point is sexual sin that profanes God's holy name.

Garments taken in pledge alludes to Exodus 22 and Deuteronomy 24, which required a poor man's cloak to be returned by nightfall; keeping it to lounge on at the altar compounds injustice with sacrilege.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Amos 2:6-16. Poetic line fragments joined into running verse text; footnote markers omitted.
